question: During the transmission of nerve impulse through a nerve fibre, the potential on the inner side of the plasma membrane has which type of electric change?
explanation: During depolarisation, the charge within the cell rapidly shifts from negative to positive. For this rapid change to take place within the interior of the cell, several events must occur along the plasma membrane of the cell as well. While the sodium potassium pump continues to work, the voltage gated ion channels that had been closed while the cell was at resting potential have been opened by an electrical stimulus. As the sodium rushes back into the cell the positive sodium ions raise the charge inside the cell from negative to positive. Once the interior of the cell becomes positively charged, depolarisation of the cell is complete.

options.text: First positive, then negative and continue to be negative
First negative, then positive and continue to be positive
First positive, then negative and again back to positive
First negative, then positive and again back to negative

question:
Assertion: The resting membrane of the neuron exhibits polarity of charges.


Reason: The outer surface of the axonal membrane possesses a negative charge while its inner surface becomes positively charged.
explanation: When a nueron is not conducting any impulse, i.e., resting, the axonal membrane is comparatively more permeable to potassium ions $\left( {{{\rm{K}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right)$ and nearly impermeable to sodium ionw $\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right)$. Similarly, the membrane is impermeable to negatively charged proteins present in the axoplasm. Consequently, the axoplasm inside the axon contains high concentration of ${{\rm{K}}^{\rm{ + }}}$ and negatively charged proteins and low concentration of ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}$. These ionic gradients across the resting membrane are maintained by the active transpoprt of ions by he sodium - potassium pump which transports ${\rm{3N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}$ outwards for ${\rm{2}}{{\rm{K}}^{\rm{ + }}}$ into the cell. As a result, the outer surface of the axonal membrane possesses a positive charge while its inner surface becomes negatively charged and therefore is polarised.

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

For this rapid change to take place within the interior of the cell, several events must occur along the plasma membrane of the cell as well. As the sodium rushes back into the cell the positive sodium ions raise the charge inside the cell from negative to positive. question: Durig the propagation of a nerve impulse, the action potential results due to
explanation: During the propagation of nerve impulse, when the stimulus of adequate strength is applied to a polarised membrane, the action potential results from the movement of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ions from extracellular fluid to intracellular fluid. This is because the permeability of the membrane to $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions is greatly increased at the point of stimulation and since $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions are more in the extracellular fluid at resting state, there occurs influx of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions through sodium ion channels by the diffusion. Since, there are more $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$entering than leaving, the electric potential of the membrane changes from $-70 \mathrm{mV}$ towards $+30 \mathrm{mV}$, i.e. action potential.
